data.title: Malaysian man arrested for forcing son, 10, to shoplift regularly
data.description:
A Malaysian man previously convicted of child abuse has been arrested again on suspicion of forcing his son, 10, to shoplift daily necessities.
Police in Bahau, in Negeri Sembilan state’s Jempol district, were alerted to what initially appeared to be a simple case of theft on Wednesday, The Sun newspaper reported. Staff from a wholesale supermarket had caught the boy trying to steal a 200-gram pack of instant coffee.
But officers soon uncovered a troubling pattern of child exploitation, Jempol police deputy chief R. Premkumar told Bernama news agency.
The boy’s 34-year-old father was believed to have been forcing him to shoplift items such as Nescafe and Milo products from outlets in the area.
“Initial investigations revealed that the suspect frequently directed and forced his eldest son to steal daily necessities from several other business premises previously,” Premkumar said.
“If the victim refused, the suspect would scold him and threaten to inflict physical harm on the child.”
Background checks revealed the father had seven previous convictions for criminal activities and drug-related offences. One of them was for child abuse, indicating that it was not the first time he had harmed children, district police chief Norhisham Mustapar told the Chinese language newspaper China Press.
“He has been on the police’s wanted list since 2021,” Norhisham said.


The man was placed in remand on Thursday for further investigation and may face charges of theft, abetting a crime and child abuse. Meanwhile, the boy’s mother has been given custody to remove him from the abusive situation.
In comments under a post about the case, social media users said they were “speechless” over the “trauma for the boy”. “I really don’t understand. Just why would he do this?” one said in reference to the father. “He’s just a kid,” another wrote.
According to Malaysia’s welfare department, nearly 5,000 children were reportedly in need of protection from abuse in 2016 alone. “Hyperactivity, disability and financial problems are just some of the everyday issues that have driven caregivers to abuse and neglect children,” Unicef said in a Malaysia report.
In May last year, after Penang’s welfare department said there had been over 4,000 child abuse cases in the state since 2010, P. Sundramoorthy, a criminologist at Universiti Sains Malaysia, told the New Straits Times that economic hardship played a big part.
“Families facing poverty and unemployment often endure elevated stress levels, which can result in neglect or abuse. The Family Stress Model explains how economic disadvantage can lead to psychological distress in caregivers, which in turn affects parenting behaviours and increases the risk of child maltreatment,” he said.
Mental health issues, lack of parenting knowledge and skills, family instability and parental substance abuse are some of the other stressors, according to Sundramoorthy.
Malaysia’s Child Act 2001 criminalises various forms of child abuse and exploitation, including physical harm and threats, forcing children to beg or engage in criminal activity, and exposing the young to situations that are harmful to their physical or emotional well-being.
Penalties vary but mostly consist of a fine, imprisonment or caning.

data.title: South Korea’s Yoon gets 5 years in prison for obstructing justice during martial law bid
data.description:
A South Korean court on Friday sentenced former president Yoon Suk-yeol to five years in prison after finding him guilty of obstruction of justice during his disastrous martial law declaration and in its chaotic aftermath, the first in a series of verdicts for the disgraced ex-leader.
Yoon briefly suspended civilian rule in South Korea for the first time in more than four decades on December 3, 2024, prompting massive protests and a showdown in parliament.
Now ousted from power, he faces multiple trials for actions taken during that debacle and in the turmoil that followed.
Among them are charges he committed obstruction of justice by allegedly excluding cabinet members from a martial law planning meeting as well as blocking investigators from detaining him.
The case mainly centres on accusations that Yoon instructed presidential security personnel to block investigators from entering his residential compound to execute an arrest warrant in January last year.
The first attempt failed after authorities encountered buses parked to block the entrance. They later succeeded in entering the compound and bringing Yoon in for questioning.
The sentencing comes days after prosecutors in a separate case demanded Yoon be sentenced to death for his role as the “ringleader of an insurrection” in orchestrating the imposition of martial law.
They argued Yoon deserved the severest possible punishment as he had shown “no remorse” for actions that threatened “constitutional order and democracy”.
If he is found guilty, it is highly unlikely the sentence will actually be carried out – South Korea has had an unofficial moratorium on executions since 1997.


Yoon was seen smiling in court as the prosecutors demanded the punishment.
And the former leader and top prosecutor has remained defiant, saying his martial law declaration was a lawful exercise of his presidential authority.
In closing remarks on Tuesday, he insisted the “exercise of a president’s constitutional emergency powers to protect the nation and uphold the constitutional order cannot be deemed an act of insurrection”.
He accused the then opposition party of having imposed an “unconstitutional dictatorship” through their control of the legislature.
“There was no other option but to awaken the people who are the sovereign.”
The court is scheduled to rule on the insurrection charges on February 19.
Yoon also faces a separate trial on charges of aiding the enemy over allegations he ordered drone flights over North Korea to bolster his case for declaring martial law.

data.title: Chinese woman swallows raw fish gallbladder, hoping to relieve headache, ends up in ICU
data.description:
A woman from eastern China ended up in the intensive care unit (ICU) hours after swallowing a raw fish gallbladder, believing it would cure her headache.
The 50-year-old woman, surnamed Liu, from Jiangsu province, adhered to the folk remedy that claimed raw fish gallbladders could clear heat, detoxify the body, and alleviate a persistent migraine that had been troubling her.
On the morning of December 14, Liu bought a 2.5kg grass carp at the market, extracted the gallbladder at home, and swallowed it raw.

Just two hours later, she began experiencing vomiting, diarrhoea, and stomach pain. Her family quickly took her to the hospital, where she was diagnosed with fish gallbladder poisoning and acute hepatic failure.
She was promptly transferred to the ICU at the Affiliated Hospital of Jiangsu University.
The medical team provided her with plasma exchange treatment and continuous renal replacement therapy, a slow blood purification method.
Five days later, Liu’s condition improved, and she was discharged from the hospital.
The case was reported by the hospital on January 7.
Doctor Hu Zhenkui stated that fish gallbladder is “even more toxic than arsenic.” He explained that only a few grams can be enough to poison a person, and gallbladders from fish weighing 5kg or more could be fatal.

He noted that the gallbladders contain toxins that can damage the liver and kidneys, potentially leading to acute liver failure and kidney failure. In severe cases, these toxins can cause shock, cerebral haemorrhage and even death.
Hu further cautioned that fish gallbladders, whether cooked or soaked in liquor – practices some believe enhance their nutritional value – remain toxic.
The hospital also advised individuals not to trust superstitious folk remedies.
Liu is not alone; there have been many cases of hospitalisation due to the consumption of raw fish gallbladders.
Patients have reported believing that this organ could help clear heat, improve eyesight and detoxify the liver.
Some even think that the gallbladder’s bitterness indicates its medicinal effectiveness.

In one case from central China’s Hubei province, a woman suffered from diarrhoea for three days after consuming a raw fish gallbladder, convinced it was a sign that her heat was being cleared.
By the time she sought medical help, serious damage had already occurred to her liver and kidneys.
“This is not health maintenance but suicide,” remarked one online observer.
“Always trust in medical advice from doctors rather than folk remedies,” advised another.
“My mum fed me raw fish gallbladders several times when I was a kid. I’m fortunate to be alive today,” shared a third.

data.title: China’s US envoy says world faces ‘stark choice’ as 2026 opens with instability
data.description:
China’s top diplomat in the United States delivered a sharply worded rebuke on Thursday over US President Donald Trump’s tumultuous approach to global relations, laying out a stark choice between a world informed by China-led stability and one battered by Washington.
Chinese ambassador Xie Feng’s comments to the 1,000-member China General Chamber of Commerce come two weeks into Trump’s second year of his latest term in the White House.
The past fortnight has already seen the Trump administration abduct Venezuelan leader Nicolas Maduro; threaten Greenland, Mexico, Colombia and Cuba; pull the US out of 61 international organisations; threaten Iran; defend an immigration agent who shot an unarmed mother; suspend immigrant visas from 75 countries; and file a criminal investigation against the US central bank chairman.
“We are just 15 days into the year 2026, but the year already has seen black swans and grey rhinos one after the other. Instability and uncertainty are raging all around,” Xie said. “Where will the world be heading? Humanity is facing a grave choice. Peace or war. Openness or isolation. Cooperation or confrontation.”
Black swan and grey rhino events refer to highly improbable, unpredictable developments that carry widespread, far-reaching consequences.


Xie addressed the chamber members hours after his embassy blasted the US over a “so-called agreement” worth up to US$500 billion to bring critical Taiwanese tech manufacturing to the US, a deal it “strongly deplored” and called on Washington to revoke.
It was also weeks after Washington approved its largest-ever arms package to the self-governed island.
Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping are scheduled to meet in Beijing in April and the growing number of irritants, following a truce established when the two met in South Korea in October, threaten to further fray ties.
The White House and the US State Department did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Xie, who has served as US ambassador since 2023, rarely used Trump’s name in the address but his intent was clear.
“The Cold War mentality has staged a comeback with power trampling rules, might challenging what is right and hegemony and bully replacing dialogue and consultation,” he said.
“As a result, the international order is facing a systematic shock, which risks sharply rising, threatening to drag the world back into the jungle.”
The seasoned diplomat sought to offer a vision of a world built on international justice with Beijing as a model. This comes as public polls and global indices show a rapid decline in Washington’s reputation and soft power, even as China notches up.
“China firmly stands on the right side of history and the progress of human civilisation,” Xie said. “While might may rule the moment, right prevails … Achieving international cooperation, sovereign equality, settling international disputes by peaceful means, are not outdated.”


The ambassador, a Jiangsu native and former commissioner of Hong Kong, spent a significant portion of his speech hammering home the “Taiwan question”, citing the self-governed island as Beijing’s redline issue and blasting “external connivance and support”, in another clear reference to the US.
Beijing sees Taiwan as part of China to be reunited by force if necessary. Most countries, including the US, do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state, but Washington is opposed to any attempt to take the self-governed island by force and is committed by law to supplying it with weapons.
Xie rattled off a host of trade, economic and social statistics, positing that China was open for business and welcomed imports from overseas partners, although it just recorded its largest trade surplus in history as it exports overcapacity and protectionism grows.
“Cooperation is better than a zero-sum game and stability is better than volatility, be it a tariff war, a trade war or a tech war. Each will hit a dead end and serve no one’s interest,” he said.
“When China and the United States work together, the world fares well. But if we confront one another, the world would suffer. We are sincere about developing China-US relations. At the same time, we have our principles to safeguard.”

data.title: Hong Kong’s Chris Tang defends police’s ‘difficult decision’ to shoot knife-wielding man
data.description:
Hong Kong’s security chief has defended police’s “difficult decision” to shoot a man who brandished a knife and took a woman hostage in a shopping centre, praising their bravery and decisiveness in saving lives.
After police fatally shot the 34-year-old man on Thursday, Security Secretary Chris Tang Ping-keung revealed the suspect was mentally unstable at the time, possibly influenced by drugs, with suspected substances found in his possession.
“It is obvious that [the suspect] was acting insane at the time,” Tang told a radio programme on Friday, adding the critical call had saved lives and stopped the suspect from hurting residents.
“It was a very difficult decision to shoot, but they were very brave. They were very decisive and I think our regular training is highly relevant to it.”
The shooting unfolded around 7pm on Thursday as the 34-year-old man broke into Don Don Donki supermarket in the centre and grabbed a knife from a kitchen counter. He waved at pedestrians, sparking panic as crowds fled.
An officer fired a burst of pepper spray, but the suspect refused to drop the weapon.
Video clips show him rushing the crowds at the centre exit, snatching a woman, grabbing her bag and holding her against his chest.
The force said that after a second verbal warning was ignored, two officers each fired one shot at the man, one hitting his chest, the other his right shoulder.
Tang said the officers were able to aim and shoot at the suspect’s body in such a critical moment thanks to force rehearsals and training on handling emergencies, especially where a suspect is about to stab a victim.

The woman was rescued and taken to hospital for treatment. The suspect received first aid at the scene before being rushed to Tuen Mun Hospital, where he was pronounced dead at 8.40pm.
The Post contacted the Social Service Department to ask if the suspect was an active case under its monitoring.
As a Post reporter visited Tuen Mun Plaza earlier in the day, the centre had blocked the shooting scene with large boards, while the exit to the site remained sealed off until around 10.40am.
A sign reading “Closed”, meanwhile, was hung at the Don Don Donki supermarket kitchen, where an online video allegedly showed a man grabbing a knife.
The kitchen had an open design, accessible through a waist-level door from the front or back.
An 18-year-old man, surnamed Kan, who lives and works nearby, said he was inside the shopping centre when the shooting occurred.
“I only heard gunshots. I had no idea what was going on at the time,” he said. “It was only after seeing the news that I learned someone was brandishing a knife and trying to stab someone,” he said.
Food and beverage worker Kay Lau, 33, who works in the area, did not witness the incident but urged colleagues to stay alert after hearing about it.
“I was really worried for my colleagues last night and called them to check; thankfully, they are safe,” he said. “This downtown area is supposed to be safe; no one expected something like this to happen.”
Lau said that while he generally felt safe in the neighbourhood, he had noticed a decline in law and order, hoping police would increase patrols.

data.title: Young Hong Kong fencers win 4 medals at FIE Junior and Cadet Epee World Cup in Bahrain
data.description:
Hong Kong’s young fencers have matched their senior counterparts by winning a host of medals at the FIE Junior and Cadet Epee World Cup in Bahrain on Thursday.
Days after the men’s team claimed foil gold in Paris, the city’s 10-strong youth squad finished with two golds, one silver and one bronze in Manama, the capital of the island nation.
Picking up where he left off a fortnight ago in Switzerland, Wong Pak-yiu beat teammate Anthony Ho Shing-fung to seal his second consecutive World Cup gold medal, while Vivienne Lee Man-wai also took the title in the girl’s event.
Her compatriot Ivana Lee claimed bronze after losing in the semi-finals.
Wong, who won gold in Basel at the turn of the year, was among the six boys and four girls from the city involved on Thursday.












View this post on Instagram























A post shared by Bahraini Fencing Association (@bah.fencing)



The 15-year-old went unbeaten in the pool stage to earn his top seed status, and then swept aside opponents from Belarus, Egypt, Turkey and teammate Song Hang to reach the final.
Ho, who was seeded sixth after losing one of his six pool stage bouts, got the better of young epeeists from Egypt, China, Australia and fellow Hongkonger Curtis Chui Kai-chit in the last four.
Wong though proved too good in the battle for gold, beating his 16-year-old rival 15-10.
The teenagers, together with 10 others from the city, will take the confidence of those performances into Friday’s junior event, which is also at Khalifa Sports City.
In the girls’ event, all four Hongkongers – Vivienne Lee, Ivana Lee, Choi Chi-yuet and Yu Tai-zhen – progressed from the pool stage.
Vivienne Lee reached the final to beat Sanam Nazarova 15-13 to clinch the title, after the Uzbekistan fencer ousted Ivana Lee 15-9 in the semi-finals. Choi and Yu finished 11th and 19th, respectively.
The women’s quartet, plus Nicole Tang Sze-nga, will next compete at the junior event on Saturday, before the men’s and women’s junior team events on Sunday.

data.title: Carney calls for China-Canada relationship that adapts to ‘new global reality’
data.description:
Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney pitched a new China-Canada relationship that could bring security and prosperity for both nations, in talks with Chinese President Xi Jinping on Friday.
“Together we can build on the best of what this relationship has been in the past, and create a new one that adapts to [the] new global reality,” Carney said during his opening remarks, adding that it could deliver stability, security and prosperity for both nations.
Carney is on a state visit to China, the first by a Canadian leader since 2017. Both nations are at odds with the United States over President Donald Trump’s tariffs, and keen to mend fences after years of strained ties.
The Canadian leader identified agriculture, energy and finance as key areas where the two nations could make “immediate and sustained progress”.


It was the second time Xi and Carney have met in three months. During talks on the sidelines of the Apec summit in Gyeongju, South Korea in October, Xi expressed a desire to restart bilateral cooperation and to “make up for lost time”.
On Friday, Xi said he was “pleased” with progress. He said their meeting in South Korea marked a positive new direction for the relationship, and the two sides had discussed restarting cooperation across various fields in recent months.
“We are willing to continue our efforts, with a responsible attitude towards history, our people and the world, to further improve China-Canada relations and move it onto the track of healthy, stable and sustainable development,” Xi said.
Ahead of his trip, Carney pledged to diversify the Canadian economy to counter global trade disruptions. Canada’s steel, aluminium and car sectors have been hit by Trump’s tariffs, with talks between Ottawa and Washington to ease those duties suspended.
Before meeting Xi, Carney wrote on social media that more than 400,000 careers in Canada were supported by the bilateral trade relationship with China.
“Here in Beijing, I’m meeting with government and business leaders because we want to build on that – opening up new markets and creating new opportunities for Canadian workers and businesses,” he wrote.

On Thursday, Carney held talks with Premier Li Qiang and they oversaw the signing of memorandums of understanding on energy, combating crime, construction, culture, food safety, and plant and animal health.
Carney also spoke with the heads of Chinese companies including investment firm Primavera Capital Group, the world’s largest electric vehicle maker CATL, state-owned China National Petroleum Corporation, e-commerce platform Alibaba Group, and tech company Envision Group, according to Canadian media reports.
At a turbulent time for global trade, Beijing and Ottawa have in the past three months signalled that they want to repair their damaged ties.
China ranks as Canada’s second-largest trading partner, with two-way merchandise trade reaching over C$118 billion (US$84.9 billion) in 2024 – much lower than the C$761 billion two-way trade with the US but vital for Ottawa’s diversification push.
Canada-China relations have been strained for nearly a decade, hitting rock bottom in 2018 during Trump’s first term as US president, when Chinese national Meng Wanzhou, a Huawei Technologies executive, was arrested in Vancouver on a US warrant.
Beijing retaliated by detaining Canadians Michael Kovrig and Michael Spavor on spying charges, in what Ottawa called “hostage diplomacy”. The stand-off halted top-level talks for years, only ending in 2021 when a deal with US prosecutors led to the release of all three.
Tensions heated up again in 2023 when Canada expelled a Chinese diplomat in response to alleged election interference and intimidation against lawmakers, claims denied by Beijing. A public inquiry found attempts by China and other countries to interfere in the elections had minimal impact on their outcomes.


Canada is the world’s largest canola exporter and in 2024, C$4.9 billion in exports of canola products went to China – its second largest buyer, according to official data. But last year, Beijing imposed duties of nearly 76 per cent on canola seeds and 100 per cent on canola oil in retaliation for the 100 per cent electric vehicle tariff that Canada slapped on China in October 2024.
Despite that, the energy sector stands to gain. China’s appetite for Canadian crude oil is expanding, with the Asian giant emerging last year as the top buyer via Canada’s newly expanded Trans Mountain Pipeline.
And with Venezuelan oil supplies disrupted by US sanctions, Chinese refiners are reportedly eager to secure stable Canadian alternatives.
Carney was also scheduled to attend a lunch hosted by the Chinese president and is expected to speak at a gala dinner hosted by the Canada-China Business Council on Friday evening.

data.title: Chinese power stocks surge on State Grid’s record US$574 billion investment plan
data.description:
Shares of Chinese electricity and grid equipment makers rose after State Grid unveiled a 4 trillion yuan (US$574 billion) plan to upgrade the country’s power networks, as surging demand and the global race in artificial intelligence drive investment in energy infrastructure.
Baoding Tianwei Baobian Electric, which makes transformers, surged 10 per cent to 15.25 yuan in Shanghai and power equipment manufacturer Henan Pinggao Electric jumped 7.4 per cent to 21.32 yuan. Suzhou Electrical Apparatus Science Academy soared 20 per cent to 8.15 yuan in Shenzhen, while Power Construction Corp of China gained 10 per cent to 5.90 yuan. That bucked a decline in the CSI 300 Index, which slipped 0.1 per cent.
State Grid, the largest utility in China and the world, announced on Thursday that it would ramp up investment in infrastructure over the next five years, 40 per cent more than the previous plan and the biggest in the company’s history.
The funds would be used to build a “new type of power system”, with targets including adding 200 million kilowatts of renewable energy capacity annually and increasing the share of non-fossil consumption to 25 per cent by 2030, State Grid said. The company oversees electricity usage of 1.1 billion people across 88 per cent of China’s territory.
Main investments would be made in China’s western regions, where there was a surplus of power supply but a shortage of grids that could transmit the energy, according to Huatai Securities.

“The investment in non-ultra high voltage grids is expected to accelerate,” said Liu Jun, an analyst at the brokerage. “It’s clear that the weak power grids in the western region need to be upgraded against the backdrop of building a unified national power market.”
The massive investment comes as the world grapples with the enormous energy appetite of AI. The International Energy Agency estimated that from 2024 to 2030, China’s data centres would consume 170 per cent more electricity while the consumption in the US would increase 130 per cent, with both accounting for nearly 80 per cent of global growth.
However, China and the US – the world’s two AI powerhouses – face completely different pictures. China tops all countries with a third of global electricity generation volume due to aggressive government planning, while the US is facing an intensifying power shortage, prompting tech giants from Google to Meta to acquire power assets to secure supply for training their AI models.
The gap in renewable energy is also widening. In 2025 alone, China was expected to add about 97 gigawatts (GW) of average renewable power output, which was more than 10 times the projected 9.6GW to be added by the US, according to a study published in the journal RSC Sustainability in December.
Separately, China Southern Power Grid, which serves five southern provinces, including Guangdong and Hainan, said on January 5 it would invest over 24 billion yuan in grid infrastructure in the first quarter of the year, up over 20 per cent from a year earlier.
The investments echo directives by the National Development and Reform Commission and the National Energy Administration calling for upgrades to existing networks and building a next-generation power system.
UBS predicted in November that China’s power demand from AI data centres would expand at an annualised rate of 2.3 per cent from 2028 to 2030, contributing to the 12 per cent annualised growth rate of the country’s power investment during the new five-year plan.

data.title: China’s quantum warfare weapons; ‘rising star’ quits US: SCMP’s 7 highlights
data.description:
We have selected seven stories from this week’s news across Hong Kong, mainland China, the wider Asia region and beyond that resonated with our readers and shed light on topical issues. If you would like to see more of our reporting, please consider subscribing.
1. Chinese military says it is developing over 10 quantum warfare weapons
The People’s Liberation Army said more than 10 experimental quantum cyber warfare tools were “under development”, many of which were being “tested in front-line missions”, according to the official newspaper Science and Technology Daily.
2. China’s solar firms, once deemed too competitive, now face monopoly accusations

China’s efforts to rein in cutthroat price wars in the solar industry have had the unintended result of creating risks of alleged price rigging, highlighting what analysts describe as an “uneasy balance” between the government’s anti-involution campaign and anti-monopoly policies.
3. ‘No oil here’: Malaysians turn US embassy space post into foreign-policy satire
A well-intentioned US embassy post featuring a satellite image of Malaysia has sparked a distinctly local burst of self-deprecating humour, with social media users quipping in response to the picture: “Sorry, we don’t have oil.”
4. Hong Kong corporate governance activist David Webb dies at 60

David Webb, the corporate governance activist renowned for his free public database and his explosive “Enigma Network” exposé that rocked the Hong Kong stock exchange, has died aged 60 of metastatic prostate cancer.
4. Zheng Yu, ‘rising star’ in chemical engineering from MIT, quits US for China
Zheng Yu, a leading young Chinese chemical engineer has left the Massachusetts Institute of Technology to return home to Peking University. Her research focuses on special materials that allow electronic devices to understand the biological signals sent by the body.
6. Foreign, Hong Kong retailers shut mainland China stores amid fierce competition
Mainland China’s retail and retail property sectors have suffered another blow amid a fresh wave of store closures by foreign and Hong Kong brands. High-profile store closures included those of Lane Crawford, Ikea, Triumph and Zara.
7. Filipino woman worships green statue for 4 years, finds out it is animated character Shrek

A woman in the Philippines spent four years worshipping a green Buddha statue before discovering that it was actually a 3D-printed figure of the cartoon character Shrek.

data.title: Year of the Horse 2026 predictions for Rats, from their lucky colour to careers and health
data.description:
From auspicious horse-themed phrases and couplets to whether your luck is in, check out our Year of the Horse 2026 series to discover all you need to know about the coming Lunar New Year.
What does the coming Year of the Horse 2026 have in store for those born in the Year of the Rat (1936, 1948, 1960, 1972, 1984, 1996, 2008, 2020)? Renowned fortune-teller Tong Pik-ha offers her comprehensive predictions for the year.
Lucky numbers: 4, 9; lucky colours: gold, silver; lucky element: gold
Rats clash with the tai sui or the Grand Duke of Jupiter in the Year of the Horse, resulting in frequent fluctuations throughout the year. Welcoming the year by attending celebratory events can help avoid bad luck.
It is recommended to visit a temple to appease the tai sui after the start of spring and to donate blood or get your teeth cleaned in the fifth and 11th lunar months to ward off the chance of getting injured. Avoid visiting the sick or attending funerals to prevent being negatively affected by bad vibes.

Wealth
Rats will be able to enjoy career advancement in the Year of the Horse. However, due to the influence of several inauspicious stars, things will not just fall into place automatically, and Rats will need to rely on their own efforts.
There will be a curse star that causes financial loss, so it is not recommended to engage in any high-risk investments. If relatives or friends ask for loans or guarantees, consider them carefully to avoid getting into damaging financial conflicts.
Things are better for the self-employed and business owners; overall income will be relatively stable and can trend upwards. Rats who are bosses should strive for higher turnover to increase income, seize market opportunities and strengthen customer relationships to boost business growth.
For salary earners, income will remain the same, but expenses will increase, so avoid impulsive spending and reduce daily living expenses. The presence of an evil star means Rats will often find it difficult to gain recognition and appreciation from superiors no matter how hard they work.
Thorough preparation is essential for Rats who are considering switching jobs in the Year of the Horse. Avoid impulsive actions and never quit your job without a backup plan.
Relationships
Conflicts with the tai sui this year mean relationships are prone to change for Rats. For those already in a stable relationship, this year might be a good time to consider entering a new phase of life, such as preparing for marriage, to counteract the changes.
For those who cannot have a wedding, maintaining a relationship will require extra care. Avoid complaining too much to your partner. Married couples may experience frequent arguments over trivial matters. It is recommended to adopt an “absence makes the heart grow fonder” strategy, as there are fewer chances to fight the less time you spend together.
Single men seeking love should take the initiative and participate in social gatherings. Single women will be able to meet their matches at work, but if the other person already has a partner, it is crucial to take decisive action and avoid situationships.
Health

The conflict with the tai sui this year will negatively affect Rats’ health and family fortune, so it is essential to pay close attention to your physical condition. For those with chronic illnesses, it is crucial to seek medical help immediately upon feeling unwell.
The presence of a bad star makes it particularly unfavourable for travel, increasing the risk of accidents, including property loss and injuries. Purchasing travel insurance before travelling is recommended for Rats to reduce the risk of financial loss due to unforeseen circumstances.
It is recommended to visit a familiar temple to appease the tai sui after the start of spring, especially during the fifth and 11th lunar months. Donating blood or getting a dental cleaning can help improve your luck.
Are your loved ones in luck this year? See our predictions for all the zodiac signs in the Year of the Horse.
